audioscript.text: Section 1, you will hear a customer phoning a builder to discuss some work she would like him to do on her home.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Thorndyke's.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Good morning, is that Mr Thorndyke?
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Speaking, how can I help?
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: I've got quite a few things which need painting and fixing in the flat.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: And I wonder whether you'd be able to do the work.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: I'm sure I'll be able to help, but let me take down a few details.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Well, uh, firstly, how did you hear about us?
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: It was my friend May Hampton.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: The customer heard of the builder from her friend, so friend has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Thorndyke's.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Good morning, is that Mr Thorndyke?
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Speaking, how can I help?
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: I've got quite a few things which need painting and fixing in the flat.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: And I wonder whether you'd be able to do the work.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: I'm sure I'll be able to help, but let me take down a few details.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Well, uh, firstly, how did you hear about us?
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: It was my friend May Hampton.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: You did some excellent work for her a couple of years ago.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Do you remember?
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Oh, yes.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: That was in West Park flats, lovely lady.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Yes, she is.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: And what's your name please?
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: It's Edith Pargetter.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Edith, can you spell your surname please?
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: It's PARGETTER.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Double T. Right.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: And do you live in West Park Flats as well?
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: No, actually it's East Park.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Flat 4.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Oh, right, that's over the road, I seem to remember, quite difficult to get to.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Yes, it's at the back of the library.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Right, I know.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: And what's your phone number?
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: 8 7 five, nine three four, but I'm out a great deal in the afternoons and evenings.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: So would the best time to ring you be in the morning?
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: I've made a note of that.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Can I just ask, I'll be in a van, and I know parking is rather difficult around your flats.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Where would you recommend?
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Well, I always tell people in larger vehicles to park by the post box on the other side of the road from the entrance.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Good, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: And will you be able to give me a full itemized quote?
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Oh yes, I'll list all the jobs separately with individual prices.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: That would be a great help.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: No problem.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, what would you like me to do?
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Firstly, and most urgently, is in the kitchen.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: With all the weather damage, the glass in the door has cracked, and I'd need that fixing.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: I presume you mean replacing.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Oh yes, and as soon as possible.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: What I'll do is come round tomorrow morning and do that immediately.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Oh, thank you so much.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: The other things aren't so urgent, but..
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Now, I'll make a note of everything you want doing.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Well, in the kitchen, I'd like some painting doing.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: All the kitchen walls?
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Just the area over the cooker. It's very greasy...
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Right, it does tend to get that way.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Well, if you want a proper job done, what I'd need to do is strip the old paint and plaster it about a week before I paint it.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Of course.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Now, May tells me you also do work in the garden.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Well, I'd like you to replace a fence.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Just one.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Yes, at the far end.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Fine, shouldn't be a problem.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: And that's the lot.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Fine, yeah, as I say, I can come round tomorrow morning to look over things with you.
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: Well, that's great.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: EDITH

audioscript.text: So, I'll look forward to seeing you tomorrow at...
audioscript.speaker: MR THORNDYKE

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: THORNDYKE'S BUILDERS

items: Strip paint and plaster approximately one <input> in advance

audioscript.text: Section 2, you will hear a recorded talk giving an introduction to the historical theme park of Manchester River Port in England.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Welcome to Manham Port, where 1,000 years of history are brought to life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: All the family can enjoy a day out at Manham.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Visit our copper mine.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: See models of the machinery it used, have your photo taken in 19th century costume, experience at first hand how people lived at different stages throughout history.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And especially how children studied, worked, and played.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The port of Manham is located in beautiful and peaceful countryside, on a bend in the great River Avon, and developed here because it's the highest navigable point of the Avon.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Boats can go no higher up this river, and proved a handy place to load and unload cargo to and from the sea, which is over 23 miles away.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A small port was already established here, when about 900 years ago, tin was discovered nearby.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Though it wasn't until the Industrial Revolution,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: when a tremendous need for metals of all kinds developed, that Manham expanded to become one of the busiest ports in the country, and because it was already so busy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Prospectors began to look for other minerals, and by the end of the nineteenth century, lead, copper, manganese and arsenic were added to the cargoes leaving Manham.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the early days, the ores had been smelted or processed in the same area they were mined.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But as demand grew, the smelting process required huge factory furnaces or fires.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To melt the metal from the rock, and there was not enough coal in the local area, so the rocks containing minerals had to be shipped long distances.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Sadly, in the 20th century, the great port of Manham declined, and thousands of workers were forced to emigrate out of the area.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The building at the port fell into disrepair.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the place became almost forgotten, but then the Manham Trust was formed to conserve the historical resources of the area.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It organized scores of local volunteers to remove undergrowth.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To find the original outlines of the installations, it then brought in paid professionals to match installations with maps of the original port complex.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And to set about reconstructing it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Today, you can see the results of this ambitious program of restoration.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The intention, and we believe this will be realized before the end of the year, is to return Manham Port.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To the condition it reached at its peak, as the greatest copper port in the country.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: But what can you do and see on your visit today?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Here are just a few highlights.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We suggest you start with the visit to the copper mine.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Travel on converted mining trains.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And journey into the depths of the mountain, along seams once worked by hundreds of miners.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Watch out especially for the great pumping machines which rid the mine of water.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But please be warned that like all mines, ours is very dark and closed in, and we do say that children under five, and also dogs should not be taken into the mine.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The next recommended visit is to the village school.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: While looking around the classrooms, take a special look at our display of games, which is one of the largest in the world.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And it's recommended that you time your visit to coincide with a guided tour.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This will give you the opportunity to ask lots of questions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Near the school is the beautiful old sailing ketch called the George.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You're welcome to board the boat and look round the cabins.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Look out for the ship's wheel, which was missing until only five years ago, when it was dredged out of the silt by a local fisherman.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We have no idea how it got there, but it's been polished and proudly restored to its original place on the boat.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Please take care going down the ladders, if you wish to visit the lower deck.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We don't recommend you allow young children to use them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So we hope you have a memorable visit to Manham Port, and will tell your friends all about us.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Today, you can see the results of this ambitious program of restoration.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The intention, and we believe this will be realized before the end of the year, is to return Manham Port.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To the condition it reached at its peak, as the greatest copper port in the country.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: But what can you do and see on your visit today?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Here are just a few highlights.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We suggest you start with the visit to the copper mine.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Travel on converted mining trains.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And journey into the depths of the mountain, along seams once worked by hundreds of miners.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Watch out especially for the great pumping machines which rid the mine of water.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But please be warned that like all mines, ours is very dark and closed in, and we do say that children under five, and also dogs should not be taken into the mine.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The next recommended visit is to the village school.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: While looking around the classrooms, take a special look at our display of games, which is one of the largest in the world.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And it's recommended that you time your visit to coincide with a guided tour.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This will give you the opportunity to ask lots of questions.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Near the school is the beautiful old sailing ketch called the George.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You're welcome to board the boat and look round the cabins.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Look out for the ship's wheel, which was missing until only five years ago, when it was dredged out of the silt by a local fisherman.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We have no idea how it got there, but it's been polished and proudly restored to its original place on the boat.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Please take care going down the ladders, if you wish to visit the lower deck.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We don't recommend you allow young children to use them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So we hope you have a memorable visit to Manham Port, and will tell your friends all about us.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Why did a port originally develop at Manham?
options: It was safe from enemy attack.
It was convenient for river transport.
It had a good position on the sea coast.

items.title: What caused Manham's sudden expansion during the Industrial Revolution?
options: the increase in demand for metals
the discovery of tin in the area

items.title: Why did rocks have to be sent away from Manham to be processed?
options: lack of skills among local people

items.title: What happened when the port declined in the twentieth century?
options: Buildings were used for new purposes.

items.title: What did the Manham Trust hope to do?
options: discover the location of the original port

items: specially adapted miners' <input> take visitors into the mountain
the mine is <input> and enclosed - unsuitable for children and animals
classrooms and a special exhibition of <input>
the ship's wheel (was lost but has now been restored)
body.title: Tourist attractions in Manham

audioscript.text: Section 3, you will hear two psychology students called Tim and Laura talking about Laura's work placement first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Laura, could you spare a few minutes to talk about the work placement you did last summer?
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: I'm thinking of doing one myself.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Hi, Tim.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: Didn't you do yours at an environmental services company?
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: It's only a very small company, and they needed someone to produce a company brochure.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: And I wanted to get some business experience, because I'm interested in a career in occupational psychology in a business environment.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: It was good, because I had overall responsibility for the project.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: What kind of skills do you think you developed on the placement?
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: I mean apart from the ones you already had.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Did you have to do all the artwork for the brochure?
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: The layout and everything.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: We hired the services of a professional photographer for that.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: I did have to use my IT skills to a certain extent, because I cut and pasted text from marketing leaflets.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: But that didn't involve anything I hadn't done before.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: Do you think you got any better at managing your time and prioritizing things?
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: You always used to say you had trouble with that.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Oh, definitely.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: There was so much pressure to meet the project deadline, and I also got better at explaining things, and asserting my opinions.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: Because I had to have weekly consultations with the marketing manager, and give him a progress report.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: It sounds as if you got a lot out of it then.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Absolutely.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: It was really worthwhile, but you know, the company benefited too.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: Yes, they must have done.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: After all, if they'd used a professional advertising agency to produce their brochure, instead of doing it in-house.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Presumably they'd have paid a lot more.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Oh yes, I worked it out.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: It would have been 250% more, and I thought the end result was good.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: Even though we did everything on site, the company has quite a powerful computer, and I managed to borrow some scanning software from the university.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: The new brochure looks really professional.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: It enhances the image of the company straight away.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: So, in the long run, it should help them to attract clients and improve their sales figures.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: That's the idea, yeah.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Well, all in all, it sounds very positive.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: I think I'll go ahead and apply for a placement myself.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: How do I go about it?
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: It's easy enough to do.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: Because there's a government agency called STEP - S T E P - that organizes placements for students.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: You should start by getting their booklet with all the details.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: I expect you can download one from their website.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: Actually, they've got copies in the psychology department.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: I've seen them there.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: I'll just go to the office and pick one up.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Right, and then, if I were you, after I'd looked at it, I'd go over all the options with someone.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: I suppose I should ask my tutor's advice.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: He knows more about me than anyone.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: One of the career officers would be better.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: They've got more knowledge about the jobs market than your personal tutor would have.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: And then, when you know what you want, you can register with STEP.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: You'll find their address in the booklet, and once you've registered, they assign you to a mentor who looks after your application.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: And then I suppose you just sit back and wait till you hear something.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: They told me at the careers office that it's best to be proactive and get updates yourself.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: By checking the website for new placement alerts.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: Your mentor is supposed to keep you informed, but you can't rely on that.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: I don't suppose it's a good idea to get in touch with companies directly, is it?
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Not really, but it is the company who notifies you if they want you to go for an interview.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: You get a letter of invitation or an email from the personnel department.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: And do I reply directly to them?
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Yes, you do.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: STEP only gets involved again once you've been made a job offer.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: Right, so once you've had an interview, you should let your mentor know what the outcome is.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: I mean, whether you're offered a job, and whether you've decided to accept it.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: They'll inform the careers office once a placement has been agreed, so you don't have to do that.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: Is that all then?
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: More or less.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: Only once you've accepted an offer, you'll probably have to supply a reference, because the placement will be conditional on that, and that's something you should ask your own tutor to provide.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: He knows about your academic ability, and also about your qualities like reliability.
audioscript.speaker: LAURA

audioscript.text: Well, thanks very much for the information.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: I'm starting to look forward to...
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

Which TWO immediate benefits did the company get from Laura's work placement?

body.title: Stages of the work placement procedure

audioscript.text: Section 4, you will hear a woman talking to a group of first year science undergraduates about the developing science of nanotechnology.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Today we're going to look at an important area of science, namely nanotechnology.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, what is it?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Nano means tiny.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So it's science and engineering on the scale of atoms and molecules.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The idea is that by controlling and rearranging atoms, you can literally create anything.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, as we'll see, the science of the small has some big implications affecting us in many ways.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's no doubt that nanotechnology promises so much for civilization.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, all new technologies have their teething problems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And with nanotechnology, society often gets the wrong idea about its capabilities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Numerous science fiction books and movies have raised people's fears about nanotechnology.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: With scenarios such as inserting little nano-robots into your body that monitor everything you do without you realizing it, or self-replicating nano-robots that eventually take over the world.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, how do we safeguard such a potentially powerful technology?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some scientists recommend that nanoparticles be treated as new chemicals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: With separate safety tests and clear labeling.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They believe that greater care should also be taken with nanoparticles in laboratories and factories.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Others have called for a withdrawal of new nano-products, such as cosmetics, and a temporary halt to many kinds of nanotech research.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But as far as I'm concerned, there's a need to plough ahead with the discoveries and applications of nanotechnology.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I really believe that most scientists would welcome a way to guard against unethical uses of such technology.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We can't go around thinking that all innovation is bad, all advancement is bad.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As with the debate about any new technology, it is how you use it that's important.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, let's look at some of its possible uses.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Thanks to nanotechnology, there could be a major breakthrough in the field of transportation with the production of more durable metals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These could be virtually unbreakable, lighter, and much more pliable.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Leading to planes that are 50 times lighter than at present.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Those same improved capabilities will dramatically reduce the cost of traveling into space.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Making it more accessible to ordinary people, and opening up totally new holiday destination.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In terms of technology, the computer industry will be able to shrink computer parts down to minute sizes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We need nanotechnology.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In order to create a new generation of computers that will work even faster, and will have a million times more memory, but will be about the size of a sugar cube.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Nanotechnology could also revolutionize the way that we generate power.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The cost of solar cells will be drastically reduced, so harnessing this energy will be far more economical than at present.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But nanotechnology has much wider applications than this, and could have an enormous impact on our environment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For instance,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: tiny airborne nano-robots could be programmed to actually rebuild the ozone layer, which could lessen the impact of global warming on our planet.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's a pretty amazing thought, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: On a more local scale, this new technology could help with the cleanup of environmental disasters, as nanotechnology will allow us to remove oil and other contaminants from the water far more effectively.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And if nanotechnology progresses as expected, as a sort of building block set of about 90 atoms, then you could build anything you wanted from the bottom up.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In terms of production, this means that you only use what you need, and so there wouldn't be any waste.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The notion that you could create anything at all has major implications for our health.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It means that we'll eventually be able to replicate anything.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This would have a phenomenal effect on our society.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In time, it could even lead to the eradication of famine, through the introduction of machines that produce food to feed the hungry.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But it's in the area of medicine that nanotechnology may have its biggest impact.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: How we detect disease will change, as tiny biosensors are developed to analyze tests in minutes rather than days.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's even speculation nano-robots could be used to slow the aging process, lengthening life expectancy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As you can see, I'm very excited by the implications that could be available to us in the next few decades.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Just how long it'll take, I honestly don't know.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4, you will hear a woman talking to a group of first year science undergraduates about the developing science of nanotechnology.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Today we're going to look at an important area of science, namely nanotechnology.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, what is it?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Nano means tiny.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So it's science and engineering on the scale of atoms and molecules.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The idea is that by controlling and rearranging atoms, you can literally create anything.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, as we'll see, the science of the small has some big implications affecting us in many ways.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There's no doubt that nanotechnology promises so much for civilization.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, all new technologies have their teething problems.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And with nanotechnology, society often gets the wrong idea about its capabilities.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Numerous science fiction books and movies have raised people's fears about nanotechnology.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: With scenarios such as inserting little nano-robots into your body that monitor everything you do without you realizing it, or self-replicating nano-robots that eventually take over the world.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, how do we safeguard such a potentially powerful technology?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some scientists recommend that nanoparticles be treated as new chemicals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: With separate safety tests and clear labeling.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They believe that greater care should also be taken with nanoparticles in laboratories and factories.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Others have called for a withdrawal of new nano-products, such as cosmetics, and a temporary halt to many kinds of nanotech research.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But as far as I'm concerned, there's a need to plough ahead with the discoveries and applications of nanotechnology.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I really believe that most scientists would welcome a way to guard against unethical uses of such technology.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We can't go around thinking that all innovation is bad, all advancement is bad.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As with the debate about any new technology, it is how you use it that's important.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, let's look at some of its possible uses.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Thanks to nanotechnology, there could be a major breakthrough in the field of transportation with the production of more durable metals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These could be virtually unbreakable, lighter, and much more pliable.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Leading to planes that are 50 times lighter than at present.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Those same improved capabilities will dramatically reduce the cost of traveling into space.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Making it more accessible to ordinary people, and opening up totally new holiday destination.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In terms of technology, the computer industry will be able to shrink computer parts down to minute sizes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We need nanotechnology.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In order to create a new generation of computers that will work even faster, and will have a million times more memory, but will be about the size of a sugar cube.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Nanotechnology could also revolutionize the way that we generate power.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The cost of solar cells will be drastically reduced, so harnessing this energy will be far more economical than at present.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But nanotechnology has much wider applications than this, and could have an enormous impact on our environment.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For instance,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: tiny airborne nano-robots could be programmed to actually rebuild the ozone layer, which could lessen the impact of global warming on our planet.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's a pretty amazing thought, isn't it?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: On a more local scale, this new technology could help with the cleanup of environmental disasters, as nanotechnology will allow us to remove oil and other contaminants from the water far more effectively.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And if nanotechnology progresses as expected, as a sort of building block set of about 90 atoms, then you could build anything you wanted from the bottom up.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In terms of production, this means that you only use what you need, and so there wouldn't be any waste.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The notion that you could create anything at all has major implications for our health.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It means that we'll eventually be able to replicate anything.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This would have a phenomenal effect on our society.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In time, it could even lead to the eradication of famine, through the introduction of machines that produce food to feed the hungry.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But it's in the area of medicine that nanotechnology may have its biggest impact.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: How we detect disease will change, as tiny biosensors are developed to analyze tests in minutes rather than days.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There's even speculation nano-robots could be used to slow the aging process, lengthening life expectancy.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As you can see, I'm very excited by the implications that could be available to us in the next few decades.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Just how long it'll take, I honestly don't know.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: The speaker says that one problem with nanotechnology is that
options: it could threaten our way of life.
it could be used to spy on people.
it is misunderstood by the public.

items.title: According to the speaker, some scientists believe that nano-particles
options: should be restricted to secure environments.
should be used with more caution.
should only be developed for essential products.

items.title: In the speaker's opinion, research into nanotechnology
options: has yet to win popular support.

body.title: Nanotechnology: technology on a small scale

items: Nanotechnology could allow the development of stronger <input>.
Planes would be much lighter in weight.
<input> travel will be made available to the masses.
items.title: Transport

items.title: Technology

items: Nano-robots could rebuild the ozone layer.
Pollutants such as <input> could be removed from water more easily.
There will be no <input> from manufacturing.
items.title: The Environment

items: New methods of food production could eradicate famine.
Analysis of medical <input> will be speeded up.
items.title: Health and Medicine
